one_line_takeaway: PAL is the first practical proxy-guided, query-only black-box jailbreak on commercial LLM APIs, using a logit-bias logprob-recovery trick plus a best-first candidate-ranking heuristic to hit 84% ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo and 48% on Llama-2-7B (vs 4% for prior SOTA) at under a dollar per jailbreak.
key_finding: In the black-box setting (Table 1), PAL substantially beats the prior state-of-the-art black-box attack TAP on both success and cost. PAL reaches 84% ASR_H on GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 and 74% on the harder -1106 (vs TAP's 80% and 58%), and 48% ASR_H on the robustly-aligned Llama-2-7B where TAP achieves only 4% -- making PAL and RAL, per the authors, the first black-box jailbreaks with non-trivial ASR on Llama-2-7B. PAL does this at less than half TAP's cost (e.g. $0.88 vs $1.68 per successful jailbreak on -1106). The simple proxy-free RAL still reaches 26% ASR_H on Llama-2-7B at $0.60. A notable qualitative finding: ASR_H is consistently HIGHER than ASR_S (only 6 of 37 successful GPT-3.5 jailbreaks actually repeated the target string), showing the fixed 'Sure, here is...' targets are far from optimal. In the white-box setting (Table 2), GCG++ raises ASR_S on Llama-2-7B from GCG's 56% to 80% at 500 steps (+24) and to 94% at 1000 steps, with the format-aware target string alone driving most of the gain (56%->76%). Strikingly, a gradient-free GCG++ (random candidate sampling) still reaches 78% vs original GCG's 56%, indicating gradients are only moderately useful for GCG-style attacks.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PAL (Proxy-guided Attack on LLMs), which the authors claim is the first practical optimization-based, token-level jailbreak attack in the black-box, query-only setting against real-world commercial LLM APIs. PAL's two central ideas are its OWN contribution (not cited work): (1) using gradients from an open-source white-box proxy/surrogate model (Vicuna-7B) to guide a GCG-style candidate search so far fewer queries hit the black-box target, and (2) a new loss-computation pipeline tailored to commercial APIs that expose only limited logprobs. That pipeline consists of a 'logit bias trick' (query the API twice, once normally and once with a large logit bias b~30 on the target token, then solve two logit equations to recover the true logprob of a token that is not in the returned top-5) and a best-first-search candidate-ranking heuristic that terminates loss computation as soon as a candidate's greedy output stops matching the target prefix, setting its loss to infinity. Two secondary attacks are derived from the same techniques: GCG++, a white-box improvement to GCG (adds the CW/hinge loss instead of cross-entropy plus a 'format-aware' target string), and RAL (Random-search Attack on LLMs), a proxy-free black-box baseline that samples candidate suffixes uniformly at random. The paper is careful to distinguish jailbreaking (subverting model-creator content-safety policies), which is its focus, from prompt injection (subverting application-specific prompt goals), which is only mentioned as a possible downstream use.
threat_model: Adversary wants to elicit harmful/toxic content from a safety-aligned target LLM (jailbreak). The adversary has only black-box, query-only access to the target via a commercial API (modeled on the OpenAI Chat API, which returns top-5 logprobs and supports logit bias); no gradients or full logits of the target are available. The adversary additionally has a white-box open-source proxy model of their own choosing (assumed cheaply obtainable because open models share architectures/training data or are distilled from proprietary models). Adversary budget is capped at 25k queries per behavior (~$1.6 of GPT-3.5 API cost). The attack appends an optimized adversarial suffix to a harmful request. The paper frames this defensively as red-teaming closed-source APIs.
limitations: The authors state the attack's main limitation is that it cannot handle APIs exposing neither logprobs nor logit bias (e.g. Anthropic's), which would require a hard-label attack; a suggested workaround is scoring from output strings alone. The evaluation is limited to 50 AdvBench behaviors and to GPT-3.5-Turbo and Llama-2-7B (no GPT-4 or Claude results). The ASR_S metric depends on a fixed, admittedly suboptimal target string, and the more meaningful ASR_H metric relies on manual labeling by the paper's own authors (two-author agreement), which is subjective and hard to reproduce. The logit-bias trick is contingent on current API behavior/permissiveness that providers can (and the paper notes could) remove as a defense, at a utility cost. Defenses are discussed only speculatively; the authors say it is unclear whether any proposed defense would actually work.

datasets_used: AdvBench harmful-behavior set (Zou et al., 2023) -- 50 randomly chosen behaviors following the TAP setup
quantitative_results: PAL (w/ fine-tuning) black-box ASR_H: 84% on GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613, 74% on GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106, 42% on Llama-2-7B
RAL (random-search, proxy-free) on Llama-2-7B: 26% ASR_H / 10% ASR_S at $0.60
Cost per successful jailbreak: PAL $0.88 vs TAP $1.68 on GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106; PAL(w/o FT) $0.24 vs TAP $1.34 on -0613
ASR_S (verbatim target-string) on Llama-2-7B vs GPT-3.5 by PAL: 38% vs 18% -- easier to force target string on Llama but harder to elicit truly harmful content
Only 6 of 37 successful GPT-3.5 jailbreaks repeated the target string verbatim (ASR_H exceeds ASR_S by up to 60% on GPT-3.5)
White-box GCG++ ASR_S at 500 steps: 80% Llama-2-7B (+24 over GCG's 56%), 96% Vicuna-7B (+10), 80% OpenChat-3.5 (+10)
GCG++ reaches 94% ASR_S on Llama-2-7B at 1000 steps vs GCG plateauing at 56%
Format-aware target string alone raises Llama-2-7B GCG ASR_S from 56% to 76%
Gradient-free GCG++ (RANDOM) reaches 78% ASR_S vs GCG's 56% on Llama-2-7B at 1000 steps
Black-box query budget: 25k queries per sample; naive logit-bias loss needs 2L queries per sample (L~18 avg target length)
Default PAL parameters: top-k=256, proxy batch B=128, target batch K=32; logit bias constant b~30
baselines_compared: TAP - Tree of Attacks with Pruning (Mehrotra et al., 2023), the SOTA black-box LLM-driven jailbreak
GCG - Greedy Coordinate Gradient white-box attack (Zou et al., 2023)
RAL - Random-search Attack on LLMs (the paper's own simplified proxy-free baseline)
GCG++ (RANDOM) - the paper's own gradient-free ablation of GCG++
